metadata.title:
RIPS RAGS OFF


RACE GAMBLING

Rev. Rasmus Shows City Council and Fair
Management No Mercy@-- Endorses Stand


Taken by The Press.

pages.raw_text:
arrangements protest that 1t would
lose $25 900 on the fair unless pool
selling is allowed, Rev. Rasmus
used the startling illustration of a
robber who had accumulated $4,000
through crime, pleading to court
when apprehended that he had $780,
900 invested in tools and apparatus
which he would lose II prevented
from following his criminal career.
His application of the theory in
volved made no difference in the
methods. One was as justifiable
as the other.


In closing, Rev. Rasmus asked
that the Z councilmen who voted
for the Interstate fair amendment
and the acting mayor who signed it,
be remembered according to their
deserts, and also that COuncilmen
Laumer, Funk and Dalke, who
voted against the amendment, sup
porting the ordinance as originally
passed, be not forgotten for the
stand they took.


In mentioning those who sup
ported and those who passed the
fair amendment, Rev. Rasmus said:


They have offended against ev-
cry mother with a child; against
every father with a son who Is
anxious to protect his son. They
have offended against every young
man who takes the first lesson in
gambling at the fair. They have Of
Tended against the laws of God.
The greatest criminal is not the
man who steals bread, or the wom-
an outcast from society. 1t is he
who brings damnation to an i'm
mortal soul"


The ministers suggestion that ev-
cry Christian society, and every
other organization interested in the
welfare of the city, stay away from
the fair was enthusiastically ap-
plauded.
